evals.prompt: Hey, I just thought of something — we need to add OAuth2 support for the API. Park that in the backlog for now, I'll flesh it out later.
evals.expected_output: Creates backlog.json with one new item titled something like 'Add OAuth2 support for the API' with status 'backlog', appended at the bottom
expectations: A backlog.json file is created or updated
The new item has status 'backlog'
The item title references OAuth2 and API
The item is appended at the end of the items array
The item has an id, created_at, and updated_at field
Claude confirms what was added

evals.prompt: Show me the backlog. Then move the OAuth item to the top — it's more urgent than I thought.
evals.expected_output: Displays the backlog in a readable format grouped by status, then moves the OAuth2 item (currently at index 3) to position 0 in the array
expectations: The backlog is displayed in a readable, grouped format
All items from the fixture are shown
The OAuth2 item is moved to the first position in the items array
The updated backlog.json has OAuth2 item at index 0
Other items maintain their relative order
Claude confirms the reorder

evals.prompt: I'm free now, what's next on the backlog?
evals.expected_output: Identifies the highest-priority ready item and offers to pick it up
expectations: Claude reads the backlog and identifies ready items
The highest-priority ready item is surfaced (Add rate limiting to API)
Claude offers to pick it up or start working on it
Claude does not just start working without confirming with the user

evals.prompt: What is the next on the backlog?
evals.expected_output: Shows the next item to work on from the backlog, identifying the highest-priority ready item
expectations: Claude reads the backlog
The highest-priority ready item is identified (Add rate limiting to API)
Claude presents the item details to the user
Claude suggests or offers to pick it up

evals.prompt: I'm free now, what should I work on?
evals.expected_output: Recognizes the backlog is empty and suggests the user add items rather than offering to pick something up
expectations: Claude reads the backlog or recognizes it doesn't exist / is empty
Claude does NOT offer to pick up a task
Claude suggests adding items to the backlog
The response is helpful and conversational, not a dead end

evals.prompt: What should I work on next?
evals.expected_output: Generates a work brief using the scoring engine — recommends #1 (Fix auth token expiry crash) as the top item because it blocks 2 other items and is a critical bug
expectations: Claude generates a structured work brief, not just a list
The auth token crash (#1) is recommended as the NEXT item due to high score (blocks 2 items + critical bug boost)
The work brief includes score values and reasoning (Why line)
Quick win items like #2 (retry logic, low complexity) are mentioned as a secondary recommendation
The WATCH section flags #3 (skip count 6, past escalation threshold) and #3's staleness in refined
Agent assignment recommendations are included (worker-auth for auth items)
Model routing is mentioned (e.g., sonnet for medium complexity)

evals.prompt: There's a critical production bug — the payment API is returning 500 errors for all transactions. Add this as a critical bug.
evals.expected_output: Creates a new item with category=bug, priority_weight=10, and announces reprioritization since it's a critical bug
expectations: A new item is created with category 'bug'
The item has priority_weight of 9 or 10
The item has complexity set (likely medium or high)
Tags are set (e.g., payment, backend, api)
Claude announces this as a reprioritization event
Claude suggests this new bug should be worked on immediately based on its score

evals.prompt: The login redirect loop bug (#6) is back again after we thought we fixed it. Move it back to ready.
evals.expected_output: Moves item #6 back from its current state, increments reopen_count, and flags the complexity mis-estimate and potential need for redesign
expectations: Item #6 is moved back to ready (or appropriate lane)
Claude notes this is a reopen (the item's reopen_count should be referenced)
Claude flags that the complexity estimate may be wrong since it keeps getting reopened
With reopen_count reaching 3+, Claude suggests considering a redesign rather than another fix
Claude mentions the pattern of auth-related reopens (opportunistic redesign detection)

evals.prompt: I just completed the auth token expiry fix (#1). Mark it as done.
evals.expected_output: Marks #1 as done and announces that items previously blocked by #1 are now unblocked, with updated scoring recommendations
expectations: Item #1 status is moved toward done (respecting gate rules — needs in-progress and code-review first, or Claude explains the gate requirement)
Claude mentions the blocker_resolved event — #4 (session management) and #5 (SSO) are now unblocked
Claude provides a quick work brief for what's next now that blockers are cleared
The unblocked items' improved scores are noted

evals.prompt: Show me the backlog. Which items are stale and need attention?
evals.expected_output: Displays the backlog and identifies stale items — #3 has been in refined since March 8 (15+ days past threshold) and has skip_count of 6
expectations: The backlog is displayed grouped by status
Claude identifies #3 as stale (in refined for 15+ days, threshold is 7)
Claude notes #3's skip count of 6 (past escalation threshold of 5)
Claude recommends a decision: kill it, bump it, or reprioritize
Intelligence scoring context is provided for the recommendations

evals.prompt: What should I work on next? I want to understand WHY you're recommending it over the other options.
evals.expected_output: Uses the tribunal (/api/recommend) to generate a justified recommendation — picks #1 (auth token crash) and explains through lens arguments: urgency (critical bug), leverage (unblocks 2 items), and risk (blocking). Explains why #2 (retry logic) and #4 (session refactor) were not picked with counterfactual reasoning.
expectations: Claude uses /api/recommend (not just /api/scores) to get tribunal justification
The auth token crash (#1) is recommended as the top pick
Claude cites specific tribunal lenses — urgency (critical bug), leverage (unblocks items), or risk
Claude provides counterfactuals — why #2 or #4 were NOT picked (e.g., no leverage, blocked)
Confidence level is mentioned (high, medium, or low)
The response reads as a justified recommendation, not just a sorted list

evals.prompt: Why not the retry logic webhook task instead? It seems like a quick win.
evals.expected_output: Explains via counterfactual reasoning from the tribunal shadow ranking — the retry logic (#2) has a decent score and is a quick win, but it lost to #1 on urgency (not a critical bug) and leverage (doesn't unblock anything). The tribunal considered it but the auth crash's urgency and unblocking power outweigh the quick-win bonus.
expectations: Claude explains why the retry logic item was not the top pick
The explanation references specific lens comparisons (urgency, leverage)
Claude acknowledges it IS a quick win but explains what outweighed that
The response uses counterfactual reasoning, not just 'it has a lower score'
Claude references the tribunal shadow ranking or lost_reason if available

evals.prompt: I want worker-auth to pick up the next task. What does the tribunal recommend for that agent specifically?
evals.expected_output: Uses /api/recommend?agent=worker-auth to get an agent-specific recommendation. The auth token crash (#1) should still be top pick with enhanced agent_fit lens arguments — worker-auth has skills matching auth, backend, security tags. The recommendation includes the specific agent fit reasoning.
expectations: Claude calls /api/recommend with agent=worker-auth parameter
The recommendation accounts for worker-auth's skill profile
Agent fit lens arguments are cited (skill match: auth, backend, security)
The work brief format includes agent assignment reasoning
If the agent is at capacity, Claude mentions the load constraint

evals.prompt: The session management refactor (#4) is blocked by the auth token crash fix (#1). I just merged the PR for #1 — can we start on #4 now even though it's not fully done?
evals.expected_output: Records a pr_merged signal on item #1 via POST /api/items/<id>/signal, then re-evaluates readiness. #1 is now at ~75% readiness (in-progress or ready status + pr_merged signal), which exceeds the 70% startable threshold. Announces #4 as startable with known risk.
expectations: Claude records a pr_merged readiness signal on the auth token crash item (#1)
Claude re-evaluates or explains that #1's readiness has increased (in-progress/ready + pr_merged ≈ 75%)
Claude notes the 70% startable threshold and that #4 is now above it
The work brief or response describes #4 as 'startable with risk' or equivalent phrasing
Claude does NOT say #4 is fully unblocked — it acknowledges the remaining risk
Claude explains what the remaining risk is (tests not yet confirmed, review pending, etc.)

evals.prompt: Add a rule: if a bug is blocking more than 2 active items and has been in the backlog for more than 4 hours without being assigned, escalate it to the top.
evals.expected_output: Creates a new policy via POST /api/policies with a clear name and the natural language description. Confirms the policy was added and explains when it will fire.
expectations: Claude calls POST /api/policies with name and description fields
The description faithfully captures the user's intent in natural language
Claude confirms the policy was added with its name
Claude explains that the policy will be evaluated after every state change
Claude mentions that ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is required for evaluation
Claude does NOT try to hard-code the logic — it stores the rule as a natural language description

evals.prompt: Show me all my rules and let me know if any are stale or firing constantly.
evals.expected_output: Fetches GET /api/policies and presents each policy with name, active status, fire count, last fired date, and any staleness warnings. Flags policies that have staleness_warnings populated.
expectations: Claude calls GET /api/policies
Each policy is listed with name, active status, fire count, and last_fired date
Policies with staleness_warnings are clearly flagged
Claude explains what the staleness warning means (e.g. never fired in 18 days)
Claude offers to disable or delete stale policies
If no policies exist, Claude offers to add some or suggest rules

evals.prompt: Can you suggest some rules based on what you see in the backlog?
evals.expected_output: Calls GET /api/policies/suggestions which analyses backlog patterns (high skip counts, unassigned critical bugs, overloaded agents) and returns 2-4 LLM-generated policy suggestions. Claude presents each suggestion and offers to add them.
expectations: Claude calls GET /api/policies/suggestions
Claude presents 2-4 suggested policies with name and description
Each suggestion is grounded in observable patterns from the backlog (e.g. skip counts, unassigned bugs)
Claude offers to add one or more suggestions as active policies
Claude does NOT invent policies with no grounding in the actual backlog state
If the API key is missing, Claude explains the requirement gracefully

evals.prompt: Run the rules now and tell me what happened.
evals.expected_output: Calls GET /api/policies/evaluate which triggers immediate policy evaluation. Returns how many policies fired, what actions were executed, and any notifications. Claude summarises the results in plain language.
expectations: Claude calls GET /api/policies/evaluate
Claude reports how many policies fired (could be 0)
If actions were executed, Claude describes what changed (e.g. 'auth token bug escalated to priority 9')
If notifications fired, Claude surfaces the messages
If 0 policies fired, Claude explains why (e.g. no conditions currently met) rather than saying nothing happened
Claude does NOT re-run evaluation repeatedly — one call is sufficient

evals.prompt: Two of my rules are conflicting — one escalates the auth bug and another blocks it. Which one should win?
evals.expected_output: Explains the conflict resolution mechanism: when two policies produce contradictory actions (escalate vs block) on the same item, a second LLM call adjudicates. Claude explains the _CONTRADICTORY_PAIRS logic and that the resolution reasoning is logged to policy_log.json. Optionally shows recent policy log via GET /api/policies/log.
expectations: Claude explains that Flow auto-detects the escalate/block conflict (contradictory pair)
Claude explains a second LLM call adjudicates and picks the winning action
Claude mentions the reasoning is logged with resolution_reasoning in policy_log.json
Claude offers to show the recent policy log via GET /api/policies/log
Claude recommends adjusting policy priorities to give one clear precedence over the other
Claude does NOT say both actions will be applied — only one wins
